meta.name: The Daily Reflection Tree
meta.description: A deterministic end-of-day reflection tool covering three psychological axes: Locus of Control, Contribution Orientation, and Radius of Concern.

nodes.text:
Good evening.

This space is yours — no performance, no judgment. Just an honest look at the day you actually had.

Let's begin.
Before we dig in — if today had to be one word, what would it be?
options: Productive
Tough
Mixed
Frustrating
Surprising

nodes.text: You said "{A1_OPEN.answer}". When something went well today, what do you think actually made it happen?
options: I prepared well or thought it through
I adapted when things didn't go to plan
The team came through for me
It just fell into place on its own

nodes.text: You said "{A1_OPEN.answer}". When things got hard, what was your first instinct?
options: Figure out what I could still do something about
Wait for the situation or someone else to shift
Push through alone, head down
Feel stuck — like there was no good option

nodes.options: answer=I prepared well or thought it through|I adapted when things didn't go to plan:A1_R_INT;answer=The team came through for me|It just fell into place on its own:A1_R_EXT_MILD

answer=Figure out what I could still do something about|Push through alone, head down:A1_R_INT;answer=Wait for the situation or someone else to shift|Feel stuck — like there was no good option:A1_R_EXT

nodes.text:
You stayed in the driver's seat today — even when things didn't cooperate.

That's not about having control over everything. It's about noticing that you still had choices. That noticing is agency — and agency is a skill that sharpens with use.

Today's wins came partly through others — that's real, and it's worth being grateful for.

But notice: you were positioned to receive what came. That's not nothing. Someone less prepared might have missed the same moment.

Tough days pull attention outward — toward what others did or didn't do, what circumstances allowed or blocked. That's deeply human.

But somewhere in today, you made at least one call. We'll look for it.
Think of one specific moment today when things didn't go as planned. In that moment, which is most true?
options: I looked for what I could do next
I wished someone would step in and fix it
I blamed the situation or the system
I took a breath and tried a different approach

nodes.options: answer=I looked for what I could do next|I took a breath and tried a different approach:A1_R2_INT;answer=I wished someone would step in and fix it|I blamed the situation or the system:A1_R2_EXT

nodes.text:
There it is. Even when things broke down, you looked for a move.

That's the internal locus in action — not optimism, not control over outcomes, just the steady habit of asking: what's still possible from here?

Wishing for rescue or blaming the system — that's not weakness. It's often a signal that you're running on empty, or that you genuinely needed support you didn't get.

Worth sitting with: was this a one-off today, or has the tank been low for a while?

You've been looking at how you handled today.

Now let's look at what you brought to it — not just effort, but intention.
Think of one significant interaction today — with a colleague, a manager, or a client. Which best describes it?
options: I helped them with something that wasn't my job
I did my part and expected the same in return
I went well beyond what was asked of me
I felt my contribution wasn't noticed or matched

nodes.options: answer=I helped them with something that wasn't my job|I went well beyond what was asked of me:A2_Q_CONTRIB;answer=I did my part and expected the same in return|I felt my contribution wasn't noticed or matched:A2_Q_ENTITLE

nodes.text: When you helped or went beyond today, what was really driving it?
options: I genuinely wanted to see them succeed
I knew it would come back to me eventually
It was simply the right thing to do in the moment
It felt good to be useful — I needed that too

nodes.text: When you felt your effort wasn't matched or noticed, how did you respond?
options: I quietly pulled back my effort
I kept going but carried the resentment
I raised it with someone directly
I reminded myself of the bigger picture and moved on

nodes.options: answer=I genuinely wanted to see them succeed|It was simply the right thing to do in the moment:A2_R_CONTRIB_PURE;answer=I knew it would come back to me eventually|It felt good to be useful — I needed that too:A2_R_CONTRIB_MIXED

answer=I raised it with someone directly|I reminded myself of the bigger picture and moved on:A2_R_ENTITLE_AWARE;answer=I quietly pulled back my effort|I kept going but carried the resentment:A2_R_ENTITLE

nodes.text:
Contributing without keeping score — that's rarer than it sounds.

Most of us give with at least one eye on the return. When you give because it's right, the work takes on a different quality. People feel it, even when they can't name it.

Giving with some self-interest in the mix isn't hypocrisy — it's honesty about how motivation works.

What matters is that you gave. The quality of the motivation can refine itself over time. It starts with the action.

You felt the pull of resentment and didn't follow it all the way. That's a harder choice than it looks from the outside.

Noticing unfairness without letting it collapse your effort — that's maturity in action.

When we feel unseen, pulling back or growing resentful is the natural response. It's also a slow leak — because it closes the loop before anything can open.

The question isn't whether you deserved the recognition. It's what you want tomorrow to look like.
Thinking about the energy you brought to work today — which is most accurate?
options: I gave more than I tracked or counted
I focused on getting my own work done well
I actively looked for ways to make someone else's day easier
I was in survival mode — just trying to get through

nodes.options: answer=I gave more than I tracked or counted|I actively looked for ways to make someone else's day easier:A2_R2_CONTRIB;answer=I focused on getting my own work done well|I was in survival mode — just trying to get through:A2_R2_ENTITLE

nodes.text:
A day where you gave more than you measured — those compound.

The people around you carry something forward. And quietly, without announcing it, so do you.

Survival mode is real. Not every day has surplus for generosity.

What's worth noticing: was today an exception, or has the tank been low for a while? That's useful data — for you, not for anyone else.

You've been looking at what you gave today.

Now let's zoom out — past yourself, to the people your day actually touched.
When you replay today in your mind, whose presence comes up most?
options: Mostly me — my tasks, my stress, my goals
My team — we were all navigating the same thing
A specific person who seemed to have it harder than me today
The people we ultimately serve — customers, users, the mission

nodes.options: answer=Mostly me — my tasks, my stress, my goals:A3_Q_SELF;answer=My team — we were all navigating the same thing|A specific person who seemed to have it harder than me today|The people we ultimately serve — customers, users, the mission:A3_Q_OTHER

nodes.text: Your own situation dominated your headspace today. Was there any moment when someone else's reality broke through that focus?
options: Yes — I noticed someone struggling, but stayed in my own lane
Yes — and I actually did something small about it
Not really — I was too deep in my own work
I was too overwhelmed to look up

nodes.text: The person or group that came to mind — what did you actually do with that awareness today?
options: I took a concrete action to support them
I held it in mind but didn't act on it
I checked in or had a real conversation with them
I let it quietly shape how I worked, even without naming it

nodes.options: answer=Yes — and I actually did something small about it:A3_R_TRANS;answer=Yes — I noticed someone struggling, but stayed in my own lane|Not really — I was too deep in my own work|I was too overwhelmed to look up:A3_R_SELF

answer=I took a concrete action to support them|I checked in or had a real conversation with them|I let it quietly shape how I worked, even without naming it:A3_R_OTHER;answer=I held it in mind but didn't act on it:A3_R_AWARE

nodes.text:
A day spent mostly in your own head — sometimes that's necessity. Sometimes it's just habit.

The radius of concern is like a muscle. Today it stayed close. That's not failure. It's just where your energy went — and worth noticing.

You noticed someone else's reality — and moved toward it, even while carrying your own weight.

That's the beginning of transcendence: the shift from 'what's happening to me?' to 'what's needed here?' It's quiet, but it compounds.

You kept others in your field of view — and acted on it. That's not sainthood. It's systems thinking applied to people.

The team, the mission, the person across the desk — they were real to you today. That makes a difference, even invisibly.

You saw it, held it, and didn't act — yet. That awareness is the first step, and it's not nothing.

The gap between noticing and acting closes with repetition. Today you did the first part.
If someone who worked alongside you today were asked 'How present were they for the people around them?' — what do you think their honest answer would be?
options: Fully with us — I could feel it
Focused on their own work, but warm when it mattered
Hard to read today — a bit distant
Genuinely looking out for everyone in the room

nodes.options: answer=Fully with us — I could feel it|Genuinely looking out for everyone in the room:A3_R2_OTHER;answer=Focused on their own work, but warm when it mattered|Hard to read today — a bit distant:A3_R2_SELF

nodes.text:
The people around you felt your presence today — not just as someone completing tasks, but as someone actually there.

That's the widest version of showing up. Most metrics miss it entirely.

You were there — but most of your bandwidth was pointed inward. That's sometimes exactly right for the day.

The question worth sitting with: was it a conscious choice, or just the default?

Three lenses. One day.

Let's bring it together.

Today you called it "{A1_OPEN.answer}".

On agency: you leaned {axis1.dominant} — {axis1.summary}.

On contribution: you leaned {axis2.dominant} — {axis2.summary}.

On radius: you leaned {axis3.dominant} — {axis3.summary}.

─────────────────────────────
{closing_reflection}
─────────────────────────────

Rest well.

See you tomorrow.

axis1.internal: you stayed close to your choices, even when circumstances pushed back
axis1.external: your attention moved toward what others did or didn't do — worth watching

axis2.contribution: you gave more than you tracked — that's the rarer orientation
axis2.entitlement: the ledger was visible to you today — what came in vs. what went out

axis3.altrocentric: you kept others in your field of view, and it shaped how you moved
axis3.self: you worked in your own lane today — sometimes that's necessary; sometimes it becomes the habit

closing.internal+contribution+altrocentric: A day where you owned your choices, gave without keeping score, and stayed connected to the people around you. Those are the days that build something lasting.
closing.internal+contribution+self: You had agency and you gave — but mostly to the task, not the people. A small pivot outward tomorrow could open something.
closing.internal+entitlement+altrocentric: You saw others clearly and kept your hands on the wheel. The contribution piece has room to grow — but the foundation is real.
closing.internal+entitlement+self: You stayed in your lane with your hands on the wheel. The next level: whose lane is adjacent, and what do they actually need?
closing.external+contribution+altrocentric: You gave and you cared — genuinely. The place to build is in how much agency you claim over what happens next.
closing.external+contribution+self: You contributed, but the day felt like it drove you. Worth asking: where did you have more room than you used?
closing.external+entitlement+altrocentric: You saw others, and you were also watching what you were owed. That tension points somewhere — probably to a tank that needs filling.
closing.external+entitlement+self: A hard day to navigate. Not every session ends with a breakthrough. Just notice it, rest, and come back tomorrow with one small thing you can own.
